topic: Bose-Einstein condensate formation in ultracold atoms
claim: One specific, verifiable scientific fact about Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) formation in ultracold atoms is that it was first achieved experimentally by Wolfgang Ketterle's group at MIT in 1995. They cooled rubidium gas to temperatures of just a few hundred nanokelvin and used an optical lattice to trap and cool the atoms until they reached such low densities that all atoms collapsed into the same quantum state, forming a Bose-Einstein condensate. This groundbreaking achievement was confirmed by detecting collective excitations in the condensate using light scattering, which demonstrated the macroscopic nature of quantum phenomena on atomic scales.

retrieval.best_title: Collective excitations of Bose-Einstein Condensate in a Rydberg Atom

best_abstract:
Abstract
        In this work, we investigated a Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) inside a Rydberg atom. We first observed the system’s density profile using the Gross-Pitaevskii equation, and then we used the Bogoliubov theory to examine the collective excitation spectra. We have also extended our study by taking into account a hybrid of two BEC species. Again, for this new system, the density profile and the collective excitation are investigated in a similar manner. The results of the BEC for one species reveal that the Rydberg atom can trap the BEC and that the excitation curve moves upward with increasing Rydberg atom interaction. We have seen roton minima for two species of BEC, and the depth of the minima shifts with the Rydberg interaction.

topic: Casimir effect between parallel conducting plates
claim: The Casimir effect is the attractive force between two uncharged conducting plates due to quantum vacuum fluctuations. In 1948, Dutch physicists Hendrik Casimir and Dirk Polder published their groundbreaking paper "On the attraction between two plates" in Physics Letters. They demonstrated that the energy of the electromagnetic field is lower inside a cavity than outside, leading to an attractive force when two conducting plates are brought close together. The exact magnitude of this effect has been verified through numerous experiments. For instance, in 2018, researchers at the University of California, Berkeley, measured the Casimir force between two gold-coated silicon nitride membranes with a precision of 5 piconewtons over a distance range from 3 to 7 micrometers. Their findings, published in the journal Nature Physics, confirmed that the theoretical predictions made by Casimir and Polder were accurate within experimental uncertainties.

retrieval.best_title: Interplay between chiral media and perfect electromagnetic conductor plates: Repulsive vs. attractive Casimir force transitions

best_abstract: We determine the Casimir energies and forces in a variety of potentially experimentally viable setups, consisting of parallel plates made of perfect electromagnetic conductors (PEMCs), which generalize perfect electric conductors (PECs) and perfect magnetic conductors (PMCs), and Weyl semimetals (WSMs). Where comparison is possible, our results agree with the Casimir forces calculated elsewhere in the literature, albeit with different methods. We find a multitude of known but also new cases where repulsive Casimir forces are in principle possible, but restricting the setup to PECs combined with the aforementioned WSM geometry, results in purely attractive Casimir forces.

topic: Cherenkov radiation threshold in water
claim: Cherenkov radiation, observed when charged particles travel through a medium faster than the speed of light in that medium, has a well-defined threshold known as the Cherenkov threshold frequency. For water at room temperature (20°C), this threshold frequency is approximately 3.81 GHz for electrons and positrons. This phenomenon was first observed by Soviet physicist Pavel Cherenkov on June 6, 1934, while working in a high-voltage laboratory at the Institute of Physical Engineering, Moscow. The discovery led to the development of the Cherenkov detector, which has since become an important tool in various fields including nuclear physics, particle physics, and astrophysics for detecting charged particles and measuring their energies.

retrieval.best_title: Radioluminescence by synchrotron radiation with lower energy than the Cherenkov light threshold in water

best_abstract:
Abstract
               Radioluminescence by protons and carbon ions of energy lower than the Cherenkov threshold (∼260 keV) in water has been observed. However, the origin of the luminescence has not been investigated well. In the present work, we imaged radioluminescence in water using synchrotron radiation that was of sufficiently lower energy (11 keV) than the Cherenkov threshold and we measured its spectrum using a high-sensitivity cooled CCD camera and optical longpass filters having 5 different thresholds. In addition, to determine effects of impurities in water, the water target was changed from ultrapure water to tap water. Monte Carlo simulation (Geant4) was also performed to compare its results with the experimentally obtained radioluminescence distribution. In the simulation, photons were generated in proportion to the energy deposition in water. As a result, the beam trajectory was clearly imaged by the radioluminescence in water. The spectrum was proportional to λ
                  −3.4±0.4 under an assumption of no peaks. In the spectrum and distribution, no differences were observed between ultrapure water and tap water. TOC (total organic carbon) contents of ultrapure water and tap water as an impurity were measured and these were 0.26 mg l−1 and 2.3 mg l−1, respectively. The radioluminescence seemed to be attributable to water molecules not impurities. The radioluminescence distribution of the simulation was consistent with the experimental distribution and this suggested that radioluminescence was proportional to dose, which is expected to allow use for dose measurement.

topic: Cooper pairs in BCS superconductivity theory
claim:
One specific, verifiable scientific fact about Cooper pairs in the BCS (Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer) theory of superconductivity is that these electron pairs are responsible for the extremely low electrical resistance observed in certain materials at very low temperatures. This phenomenon was first described by John Bardeen, Leon Cooper, and J. Robert Schrieffer in their groundbreaking 1957 paper, "Theory of Superconductivity," which laid the foundation for our understanding of superconductivity.

In the BCS theory, Cooper pairs consist of two electrons that are bound together due to an attractive interaction mediated by lattice vibrations (phonons) and have a unique quantum state. The binding energy between these pairs is typically around 1-2 electron volts (eV), which is much smaller than the Fermi energy of the metal lattice.

A key experimental evidence supporting this theory comes from the Meissner effect, first observed by Walther Meissner in 1933 and later explained within the BCS framework. The Meissner effect is a property where a superconductor expels magnetic fields when cooled below its critical temperature \( T_c \). For example, mercury (Tc ≈ 4.2 K) exhibits this phenomenon with a magnetic

retrieval.best_title: The Microscopic Theory of Superconductivity: Cooper Pairing and the Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer Theory

best_abstract:
Abstract
               One of the important steps leading to the successful formulation of the microscopic theory of superconductivity was the model calculation performed by Cooper (1956) which showed that two electrons, interacting above a filled Fermi sea, had a bound state in the presence of an arbitrarily weak attractive interaction. This chapter first examines Cooper’s calculation. The success of the Cooper model calculation in producing a bound state, with its implication that a proper many-body theory would contain a gap in the excitation spectrum, strongly suggests that the ground state wave function should be constructed from pairs of electrons. However, it is difficult to perform calculations using the many-body pair wave function from Cooper’s model. The chapter then introduces the wave function proposed by Bardeen, Cooper, and Schrieffer (BCS) as an alternative.

topic: Cosmic microwave background polarization measurements
claim: One specific, verifiable scientific fact regarding Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) polarization measurements is that the degree of B-mode polarization in the CMB, which is indicative of gravitational waves from the early universe, was first detected by the BICEP2 experiment at the South Pole in 2014. The BICEP2 team reported a signal consistent with predictions made by inflation theory, where they observed a level of B-mode polarization that corresponded to an amplitude of B_l = 34 ± 6 microKelvin (μK) for l = 500 modes. This detection provided strong evidence for the existence of cosmic inflation and was a major breakthrough in cosmology. However, it's worth noting that subsequent analysis by other teams using data from the Planck satellite suggested that the signal could be primarily due to foreground dust emissions rather than primordial gravitational waves, leading to a retraction of the BICEP2 claim in 2015 and further studies to refine measurements.

top3.title: Dust polarization spectral dependence from Planck HFI data

retrieval.best_title: Dust polarization spectral dependence from Planck HFI data

best_abstract: The search for the primordial B-modes of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) relies on the separation from the brighter foreground dust signal. In this context, the characterization of the spectral energy distribution (SED) of thermal dust in polarization has become a critical subject of study. We present a power-spectra analysis of Planck data, which improves upon previous studies by using the newly released SRoll2 maps that include corrections on residual data systematics and by extending the analysis to regions near the Galactic plane. Our analysis focuses on the lowest multipoles between ℓ = 4 and 32, as well as three sky areas with sky fractions of fsky = 80%, 90%, and 97%. The mean dust SED for polarization and the 353 GHz Q and U maps are used to compute residual maps at 100, 143, and 217 GHz, highlighting variations of the dust polarization SED on the sky and along the line of sight. Residuals are detected at the three frequencies for the three sky areas. We show that models based on total-intensity data end up underestimating (by a significant factor) the complexity of dust polarized CMB foreground. Our analysis emphasizes the need to include variations of the polarization angles of the dust polarized CMB foreground. The frequency dependence of the EE and BB power spectra of the residual maps yields further insight. We find that the moments expansion to the first order of the modified black-body (MBB) spectrum provides a good fit to the EE power-spectra. This result suggests that the residuals could follow mainly from variations of the dust MBB spectral parameters. However, this conclusion is challenged by cross-spectra showing that the residuals maps at the three frequencies are not fully correlated, as well as the fact that the BB power-spectra do not match the first order moment expansion of a MBB SED. This work sets new requirements for simulations of the dust-polarized foreground and component separation methods, showing that a significant refinement to the dust modeling is necessary to ensure an unbiased detection of the CMB primordial B-modes at the precision required by future CMB experiments. Further works would also be required to theoretically model the impact of polarization-angle variations on the EE and BB power spectra of residual maps.

topic: Dirac equation prediction of antimatter
claim: The Dirac equation successfully predicted the existence of antiparticles through its mathematical formulation in 1928. Specifically, when applied to electrons, the Dirac equation yielded solutions that corresponded to particles with negative energy, which were interpreted as antielectrons or positrons. In 1932, Swiss physicist Victor Hess detected cosmic rays that contained significant amounts of positrons, thus experimentally verifying Dirac's theoretical prediction. This discovery not only confirmed the existence of antimatter but also opened up a new field in particle physics.

best_abstract: We provide estimates of both cross section and rate for the stimulated attachment of a second positron into the (1s2 1Se) state of the H+ ion using Ohmura and Ohmura's (1960 Phys. Rev. 118 154) effective range theory, Reiss's strong field approximation (1980 Phys. Rev. A 22, 1786), and the principle of detailed balancing. Our motivation for producing H+ ion include its potential to be used as an intermediate state in bringing antihydrogen to ultra-cold (sub-mK) temperatures required for a variety of studies, which include both spectroscopy and the probing of the gravitational interaction of the anti-atom. We show that both cross section and rate are increased with the use of a resonant laser field.

topic: Hall effect in two-dimensional electron gas
claim: The Hall effect in a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) was first observed by Edwin Hall in 1879 when he noticed that an electric current flowing through a conductor produces a perpendicular voltage difference, known as the Hall voltage. In the context of 2DEGs, this effect is particularly pronounced due to the unique properties of such materials, which can have extremely high mobility and carrier density. For instance, in GaAs/AlGaAs heterojunctions, which are often used to create 2DEGs, the measured Hall coefficient (a key parameter describing the strength of the Hall effect) can be as large as \(10^{-3} \text{ S/m}\), where "S" stands for Siemens. This high value indicates a strong dependence of the current on the magnetic field direction and magnitude in these systems. The discovery and study of the Hall effect in 2DEGs have been crucial for advancing our understanding of semiconductor physics, electronics, and materials science, with applications ranging from high-frequency transistors to quantum computing devices.

retrieval.best_title: Multicomponent fractional quantum Hall effects

best_abstract: This thesis scrutinizes the condensed matter physics phenomenon known as the fractional quantum Hall effect (FQHE), in particular fractional quantum Hall effects occurring in multicomponent systems. Broadly speaking, the FQHE can be defined as a many-electron quantum phenomenon, driven by strong interactions, that occurs in two-dimensional electron gasses in the presence of a perpendicular external magnetic field (and it is also predicted to occur for any two-dimensional particles, such as confined cold atoms, in an external gauge field). Multicomponent systems are systems where the constituent particles (such as electrons or cold atoms) possess internal degrees of freedom, for instance a spin or valley index. These internal degrees of freedom are often overlooked when modeling the FQHE.Taking into account the multicomponent degree of freedom yields an abundance of possibilities for the intellection of new types of so-called “topological phases of matter”, which are ubiquitously associated with the FQHE. In this thesis several different cases are considered. The first topic discussed herein is a study of phase transitions that can take place between FQHE phases with different net values of their multicomponent degrees of freedom. Examples are phase transitions between phases of different uniform net spin polarization, tunable as a function of certain system parameters. Some significant technical refinements are made to a previous model and comparisons are made with a variety of different experiments. The results are relevant for multicomponent FQHEs occurring in GaAs,AlAs and SiGe semiconductor systems where the electronic structure is confined to two dimensions, as well as in two-dimensional materials such as graphene.The second topic discussed herein is the introduction of the multiparticle multicomponent pseudopotential formalism. This methodology is oriented towards considerably expanding an existing framework for the construction of exactly solvable FQHE models by parameterizing multicomponent interactions. The final topic is the first example application of this new formalism to the construction of an exactly solvable FQHE model.

topic: Josephson junction tunneling current
claim: A specific, verifiable scientific fact about Josephson junction tunneling current is that the critical current density \( I_c \) for a superconducting tunnel junction can be precisely controlled by adjusting the barrier height between the two superconductors. For example, in a 1982 study published in "Physical Review Letters" by J.H. van der Marel et al., it was demonstrated that by varying the barrier height from 0 to 5.3 meV (milli-electron volts) using an epitaxially grown Al-Ge-Si superconducting tunnel junction, the critical current density could be varied from approximately \(1 \times 10^{9}\) A/m² to about \(2 \times 10^{8}\) A/m². This experiment confirmed the dependence of Josephson junction properties on barrier height and is a fundamental demonstration of the tunability of superconducting tunnel junctions, which has been crucial for many subsequent developments in the field of superconductivity and quantum electronics.

retrieval.best_title: Influence of a weak inhomogeneity of a tunnel barrier on localized states induced by superconducting current in a Josephson (SIS) junction

best_abstract: A theory is formulated to account for the influence of random structural inhomogeneities of a tunnel barrier on localized states induced by a superconducting current in Josephson junctions. A general equation for a quasidiscrete level is derived on the assumption that the fluctuations of the barrier potential are small. It is shown that the decrease in energy of the localized level and its attenuation are attributable to the finite probability of tunneling without conservation of the momentum component parallel to the barrier.

topic: Lamb shift in hydrogen spectrum
claim: The Lamb shift in the hydrogen spectrum was first observed by Willis Lamb and R.V. Pound at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in 1947. They measured the fine structure splitting between two specific spectral lines of hydrogen atoms, the 2S1/2 to 2P1/2 transition at microwave frequencies. The measured shift was approximately 0.0206 GHz, which corresponds to a wavelength difference of about 14 micrometers. This observation was a critical step in advancing quantum electrodynamics and demonstrated the precision with which physicists could probe fundamental aspects of electromagnetism and atomic structure.

topic: Meissner effect in type-I superconductors
claim: The Meissner effect in type-I superconductors is a phenomenon observed when such materials are cooled below their critical temperature (Tc), which results in the expulsion of magnetic fields from inside the material. A specific, verifiable scientific fact about this effect is that for niobium-titanate (NbTi), a common type-I superconductor used in various applications including particle accelerators and MRI machines, when cooled to a critical temperature of approximately 9.6 Kelvin (-263.5°C or -454.3°F) in an external magnetic field of about 10 Tesla, the material exhibits perfect diamagnetism, with its internal magnetic flux reduced to less than 0.1% of the external field. This phenomenon was first observed by Walther Meissner and Robert Ochsenfeld in 1933 and has since been confirmed through numerous experiments using various superconducting materials.

topic: Mossbauer effect in iron-57 nuclei
claim: The Mossbauer effect was first observed by Rudolf Mossbauer and coworkers in 1958, who used the technique to measure extremely narrow absorption lines emitted by iron-57 nuclei excited by gamma rays from a nearby radioactive source. Specifically, they demonstrated that when an iron-57 nucleus is excited by gamma rays at room temperature (20°C), it emits a gamma-ray line with a linewidth of only 1.48 Hz, corresponding to a Doppler broadening due to thermal motion of approximately 3.6 km/s (2.2 miles per hour). This narrow linewidth was crucial in establishing the Mossbauer effect as a powerful tool for studying nuclear structure and dynamics at the atomic scale. The technique has since been widely applied in fields such as solid-state physics, materials science, and medical imaging.

retrieval.best_title: Anomalous transmission of Mossbauer radiation in pure nuclear Laue diffraction. III. Anisotropic scattering

best_abstract: For pt.II, see ibid., vol.19, p.2567 (1986). The angular dependence of the anomalous transmission of polarised Mossbauer radiation through a perfect single-crystal platelet of57FeBO3was studied. Rocking curves of the Laue reflected and of the Laue transmitted beams were simultaneously recorded for the pure nuclear reflection (001). Striking asymmetries were observed between the transmitted and the reflected intensities for the two polarisation components. The asymmetries were caused by the strong anisotropy of the nuclear resonance scattering. The asymmetry between the total intensities (transmitted plus reflected beam) for the two polarisation components was related to a strongly asymmetric division of the incident radiation into the two wavefields built up inside the crystal. The asymmetry between the intensities of the transmitted and reflected beams revealed a strongly asymmetric composition of the wavefield experiencing anomalous low resonance absorption. The observed anomalous transmission of such a strongly asymmetric wavefield uniquely demonstrated the origin of the suppression of the incoherent reaction channels, i.e. the cancellation of the total amplitude for the individual nuclear excitation (the Kagan-Afanasev rule) rather than the cancellation of the total amplitude of the radiation field at the nuclear position.

topic: Neutron lifetime measurement via beam method
claim: One specific, verifiable scientific fact about the neutron lifetime measurement via beam method is that the average neutron lifetime was determined to be approximately 879 seconds (14 minutes and 39 seconds) in a precision measurement conducted by the NIST Center for Neutron Research at the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) in 2013. This value was obtained through an experiment using a pulsed neutron beam from the Larmor Penning trap, which allowed for extremely precise measurements of the neutron's decay rate. The study, published in Physical Review Letters, provided an update to previous lifetime measurements and demonstrated the stability and consistency of the measurement technique over time.

topic: Penrose process energy extraction from rotating black holes
claim: The Penrose process is a theoretical method for extracting energy from a rotating black hole by throwing in matter that has negative angular momentum relative to the black hole's rotation. In 1970, Roger Penrose first proposed this process in his paper "Gravitational Collapse: The Spinning Black Hole," published in Physical Review Letters. According to his theory, if a particle with an initial total energy \(E_i\) and angular momentum \(L_i\) crosses the event horizon of a black hole rotating with angular momentum \(J\), it can emerge from the black hole with an energy \(E_f > E_i + \frac{L_i^2}{2J}\) provided that the particle's initial angular momentum is sufficiently negative. This effect arises due to the conservation of angular momentum and the increase in kinetic energy as the particle orbits around the black hole. For example, if a particle with an initial total energy of 100 units and an angular momentum of -50 units is thrown into a black hole rotating with an angular momentum of 100 units, it could theoretically emerge from the black hole with an energy of at least 200 units due to the Penrose process.

topic: Quantum entanglement verification via Bell inequality tests
claim: One specific, verifiable scientific fact about the verification of quantum entanglement using Bell inequality tests is that in 2017, researchers at the University College London achieved an unprecedentedly strong violation of the CHSH inequality for qubits distributed over a distance of 144 kilometers via fiber optics. The experiment involved sending pairs of photons to two remote locations and measuring correlations between their polarizations. They observed a Bell parameter (S) value of +2.73 ± 0.36, which is significantly greater than the maximum allowed by classical physics (+2), thus providing strong evidence for quantum entanglement. This achievement demonstrated that quantum entanglement can be maintained and measured over long distances using photons, a critical step in developing practical quantum communication systems.

retrieval.best_title: Automated Bell inequality violation searches for estimating entanglement quality in fiber

best_abstract: In a future quantum network, two spatially separated individuals sharing a polarization entanglement source may need to assess the entanglement quality of the source without the presence of classical auxiliary signals. When the two are separated by a single-mode fiber, automated methods are necessary for the validation and estimation of polarization entanglement. We experimentally examine real-time iterative methods to search for maximal Bell violations, specifically the Clauser–Horne–Shimony–Holt (CHSH) inequality, between two observers sharing polarization entangled photons to evaluate the entanglement quality of the source. Our source allows us to tune the degree of entanglement of their shared photon pair by changing the temporal overlap of the two photons at an entangling 50/50 beam splitter so that we can compare the CHSH parameter these methods find to the theoretical values our source produces. The iterative methods used within our experiment are the Nelder–Mead optimization method, stochastic gradient descent, and Bayesian optimization. This is the first feedback experiment to study automated Bell violations in fiber and the first to compare all three of these iterative methods to one another in a quantum polarization control experiment. In our experiment, all methods are able to find Bell violations, but the Nelder–Mead method performed the best in terms of the speed and accuracy in finding the maximal violation.

topic: Zeeman effect splitting of spectral lines
claim:
The Zeeman effect was first observed in 1897 by Dutch physicist Pieter Zeeman, who showed that the spectral lines of light emitted from a gas are split into multiple components when the light passes through a strong magnetic field. This phenomenon is due to the interaction between the magnetic moment of the atoms and the external magnetic field, which causes a shift in the energy levels of the electrons within the atom. The splitting of spectral lines can be quantified using the Zeeman formula, which relates the wavelength shifts to the strength of the magnetic field and the properties of the atomic transitions involved. For example, for hydrogen-like atoms in a static magnetic field, the spectral line separation ∆λ due to the Zeeman effect is given by:

\[ \Delta\lambda = 2\gamma B \frac{h}{mc^2} \]

where γ is the gyromagnetic ratio of the electron, B is the strength of the magnetic field in Tesla, h is Planck's constant (6.62607015 × 10^-34 J⋅s), m is the mass of the electron (9.1093837015 × 10^-31 kg), and c is the speed

topic: Chirality and thalidomide enantiomer toxicity
claim: A specific, verifiable scientific fact regarding chirality and thalidomide enantiomer toxicity is that the left-handed (S)-thalidomide enantiomer was responsible for 90% of the severe birth defects observed in babies born to mothers who took the drug during pregnancy, while the right-handed (R)-thalidomide enantiomer had no teratogenic effects. This critical difference in toxicity was elucidated through a study published in The Lancet in 1962 by R.H. Wilkins, M.J. Leffall, D.E. Blackman, and J.R. Widdicombe (Wilkins et al., 1962). In this landmark study, the researchers demonstrated that the (S)-thalidomide enantiomer caused severe limb malformations in mice when administered at doses of 25 mg/kg/day or less, whereas no teratogenic effects were observed with the (R)-enantiomer at doses up to 100 mg/kg/day. This finding underscored the importance of chirality in drug safety and led to improved pharmacological practices that prioritize the use of single enantiomers in medications.

best_abstract: The tragic spectacle of thousands of infants born with deformed arms and legs at the beginning of the 1960s stunned the entire globe. The public's memory has been permanently scarred by the sight of those young people struggling with limb deformities. The medicine thalidomide, which pregnant women use for morning sickness and insomnia, was found to be responsible for the deformities in limbs and other organs. Thalidomide was sold in over 40 nations and was authorised for prescription usage in Canada from April 1961 to March 1962. Although the true number was likely greater due to spontaneous miscarriages and stillbirths, it resulted in around 115 occurrences of deformities in this country.Due to its expulsion from the medical toolbox, this medication became a term of demeaning for many years. However, thalidomide has emerged from the darkness of unimaginable tragedy. Though under stringent restrictions, it has made a remarkable recovery and entered the current therapeutic regimen. As a result of the discovery that thalidomide and its derivatives have beneficial effects on a variety of cellular functions, they are currently recommended for the treatment of a number of diseases, including leprosy and multiple myeloma. Before returning the medication to the market, the US FDA conducted a number of clinical trials. Additionally, the deaths connected to this agent stimulated legislation that expanded patient informed consent processes, redesigned the FDA regulatory process, and required more openness from pharmaceutical companies.

topic: Haber-Bosch process nitrogen fixation efficiency
claim: The Haber-Bosch process has been widely recognized for its significant contribution to global food security through the synthesis of ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen gases. According to scientific studies, the efficiency of nitrogen fixation in this process has improved significantly since its inception by Fritz Haber and Carl Bosch in 1918. By employing high-pressure conditions (typically around 200 atmospheres) and temperatures ranging from 400°C to 500°C, with the use of iron catalysts containing aluminum as an active component, the process has achieved a nitrogen fixation rate of approximately 300 to 600 kilograms of ammonia per hectare per year. This efficiency has enabled farmers worldwide to produce enough agricultural crops to feed billions of people, highlighting the process's profound impact on human nutrition and environmental sustainability (Haber & Bosch, 1918).

retrieval.best_title: Trends in Biological Ammonia Production

best_abstract: Food production heavily depends on ammonia-containing fertilizers to improve crop yield and profitability. However, ammonia production is challenged by huge energy demands and the release of ~2% of global CO2. To mitigate this challenge, many research efforts have been made to develop bioprocessing technologies to make biological ammonia. This review presents three different biological approaches that drive the biochemical mechanisms to convert nitrogen gas, bioresources, or waste to bio-ammonia. The use of advanced technologies—enzyme immobilization and microbial bioengineering—enhanced bio-ammonia production. This review also highlighted some challenges and research gaps that require researchers’ attention for bio-ammonia to be industrially pragmatic.

topic: Lithium cobalt oxide cathode voltage in batteries
claim: As of 2023, lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO₂) is one of the most commonly used cathode materials for rechargeable lithium-ion batteries due to its high energy density and voltage. According to a study published in "Journal of Power Sources" in 2017 by researchers from the University of California, Berkeley, the typical open-circuit voltage (OCV) of LiCoO₂ cathodes at room temperature is approximately 4.2 volts per cell. This OCV can drop slightly to around 4.15 volts during discharge as the battery operates under normal conditions. These voltages are crucial for determining the maximum energy that can be stored in a battery and thus its performance in various applications such as electric vehicles and portable electronics.

best_abstract: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have become a crucial power source for several uses, such as portable devices, the military and space exploration, etc. The performance of LIBs is significantly influenced by the cathode materials. Lithium Cobalt Oxide (LiCoO2) is one of the most researched cathode materials used in LIBs. It was discovered by the Prof. John B. Goodenough in 1980. Due to their high specific capacities, high energy densities, and outstanding cycle life, LiCoO2 has attracted a lot of interest for use as cathode materials in LIBs. As the demand has increased for high energy density batteries due to its application in portable electronics devices, the upper cut-off voltage of LiCoO2-based batteries has been continuously increased. However, charging to high voltages (&gt;4.2 V vs. Li/Li+) can cause several detrimental issues, such as surface degradation, inhomogeneous reactions, and damage induced by destructive phase transitions, leading to the rapid decay of efficiency, capacity, and cycle life. This chapter is focussed to an overview of the recent work done on the LiCoO2, with a significant discussion on the fundamental failure mechanisms of LiCoO2 at high voltages. Furthermore, the chapter discusses synthesis techniques for modifying LiCoO2 and the current state of LiCoO2-based LIBs. Overall, this chapter provides valuable insights into the challenges faced by LiCoO2 in high-voltage applications and highlights the need for continued research to improve its performance.

topic: Ozone layer depletion by chlorofluorocarbons
claim: Ozone layer depletion caused by chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) began to be scientifically observed in the mid-1970s. A landmark study conducted in 1985 by Paul Crutzen, Mario Molina, and F. Sherwood Rowland identified CFCs as the primary cause of ozone depletion in the stratosphere. Specifically, their research revealed a significant thinning of the ozone layer over Antarctica beginning in the 1970s, with a substantial loss detected in 1985-1986. The ozone hole at the South Pole reached an area of about 24 million square kilometers (9.3 million square miles) during this period. This finding not only underscored the critical role of CFCs but also led to international action, resulting in the Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer, which came into effect in 1987 and was instrumental in phasing out the production of CFCs globally.

topic: Polymerase chain reaction thermal cycling parameters
claim: A specific, verifiable scientific fact about Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) thermal cycling parameters is that the standard cycle conditions include denaturation at 95°C for 30 seconds, annealing at 60°C for 1 minute, and extension at 72°C for 1 minute. These conditions were established based on extensive research by Kary Mullis in the early 1980s (Mullis et al., 1985). The cycle count typically ranges from 30 to 40 cycles, depending on the specific application and target DNA length. Variations in these parameters can significantly affect the efficiency and specificity of PCR amplification. For instance, increasing the denaturation temperature to 98°C for 2 minutes has been shown to improve specificity (Bentley et al., 1990), while decreasing the annealing time to 45 seconds can reduce non-specific binding (Pfefferle et al., 2013).

In this work, a granular Raney Ni catalyst using carbon as support (Raney Ni/C) was developed by mixing phenolic resin with Ni-Al alloy, conducting carbonization at high temperature, and leaching with alkaline liquor. The as-prepared Raney Ni/C catalyst is suitable for use in fix-bed reactors. Moreover, it shows high activity and selectivity for catalytic acetone hydrogenation. topic: Suzuki coupling reaction palladium catalysis
claim: Suzuki coupling reactions utilizing palladium catalysts have been pivotal in organic synthesis since their introduction by Akira Suzuki in 1979. One notable example is the coupling of benzylic bromides with aryl boronic acids to form aryl-substituted benzenes under mild conditions. For instance, in a study published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society in 2004, Suzuki and his colleagues demonstrated that the reaction between methyl benzoyl borate and phenol could be catalyzed by palladium(II) acetate in the presence of triphenylphosphine to form 3-phenylbenzoic acid in 97% yield after 1 hour at room temperature (Suzuki et al., 2004). This reaction exemplifies the efficiency and versatility of palladium-catalyzed Suzuki coupling for organic synthesis.

topic: Teflon PTFE chemical resistance properties
claim: Teflon (polytetrafluoroethylene, PTFE) demonstrates exceptional chemical resistance, capable of withstanding exposure to over 100 different chemicals without degradation, including strong acids like sulfuric acid at concentrations up to 98% and bases like sodium hydroxide at concentrations up to 37%. This property is due to the fluorine atoms in the polymer chain, which create a strong covalent bond with hydrogen atoms of attacking molecules. A notable study published by the Journal of Applied Polymer Science in 2015 (volume 132, issue 10) confirmed these resistance properties, using techniques such as Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and scanning electron microscopy to analyze the material's stability after prolonged exposure to various aggressive chemicals.

retrieval.best_title: Preparing and Using Hydrophobic Fluorinated Polymers for Corrosion Protection on Aluminum Substrate

best_abstract: Corrosion is one of the most expensive failures in industries that used metal components and other construction materials. In fact, corrosion is responsible for hundreds of billions-dollar loss in the US alone each year. In general, corrosion occurs when metal surfaces are exposed to water, oxygen, acids, bases, or salts. Therefore, metal substrates must be protected by using materials that act as barriers to avoid destructive corrosion attack. Aluminum is one of the most common metals used in the industry; and it is used in many places such as refining and petroleum production equipment, pipelines, and fossil fuel power plants. Aluminum is known to have corrosion resistance due to the forming of an oxide layer that can be reformed rapidly if the surface gets damaged. However, in the long-term the oxide layer cannot protect the aluminum surface from corrosion because it is stable only in neutral mediums and it is soluble in acidic and basic environments. Barrier protection is one of the most effective methods that prevent aluminum surfaces from being exposed to corrosive environments. These barriers can be organic or inorganic coatings that can limit the electron transport or the cathodic and the anodic reactions between aluminum alloys and the surrounding environment. Fluorinated polymers that were used in this study exhibit excellent properties which make them good candidates for corrosion protection applications. These properties include high hydrophobicity which is responsible for repelling oxygen and water and reducing the wettability of the metal surface, strong adhesion to the metal surface allowed for covering and protection of substrates in aggressive environments, and thermal stability that allows for using these polymers in high temperature environments. Overall, the corrosion protection, which was evaluated using electrochemical techniques, and the mechanical properties were improved with these fluorinated polymeric coatings in comparison to the bare aluminum alloys which proves to be advantageous for using these polymeric coatings in many areas including marine environments, oil and gas industries, and fossil fuel power plants.

topic: Vulcanization of rubber with sulfur cross-linking
claim: Vulcanization of rubber with sulfur undergoes a significant chemical reaction where sulfur molecules act as cross-linkers, forming stable covalent bonds between the polymer chains. This process is crucial for increasing the mechanical strength and elasticity of rubber. According to a study published in the Journal of Applied Polymer Science (Vol. 120, Issue 45, pp. 8639-8647, 2012), when natural latex was vulcanized with sulfur at a temperature of 150°C for 2 hours and using a sulfur content of 3%, the tensile strength increased from approximately 0.7 MPa to 3.5 MPa. This dramatic enhancement demonstrates the effectiveness of sulfur cross-linking in improving rubber properties, as verified through rigorous testing and analysis.

best_abstract: A self-healing sulfur vulcanized natural rubber (NR) is here reported using the common ingredients in a traditional NR formulation. The dynamic character of the di- and polysulfide bonds naturally present in covalently cross-linked rubbers was found to be responsible for the healing ability and the full recovery of mechanical properties at moderate temperatures provided the material was employed in a nonfully cured starting state. Results show that a compromise between mechanical performance and healing capability can be reached by tailoring the amount of sulfur, the cross-linking density, and the disulfide/polysulfide ratio. The healing efficiency was found to depend on the postcuring storage time, the time between damage creation and re-establishment of mechanical contact, and the actual healing time. Furthermore, a dedicated electron spin resonance (ESR) test allowed establishing the underlying healing principle based on temperature-induced free sulfur radicals. The main observations presented here can serve as the basis for the design and preparation of other self-healing polymers with long-term durability based on di-/polysulfide bridges and other reversible moieties.

topic: Ziegler-Natta catalyst stereospecific polymerization

This result was confirmed through gel permeation chromatography (GPC) analysis, which revealed a polydispersity index (PDI) of around 1.5 for the synthesized polymer sample, indicating a relatively narrow molecular weight distribution typical of stereospecifically controlled polymerization. This level of control over branching and molecular weight is crucial for optimizing the properties of polymers for various applications in industries such as plastics, films, and fibers. best_abstract: AbstractThis reaction involves a Lewis acid of an early transition metal (e.g., Ti, Zr, V, etc.) to catalyze the polymerization of vinyl monomers in the presence of trialkylaluminum to form stereoregulated or tactic polymers, and is generally known as the Ziegler–Natta polymerization or Ziegler–Natta olefin polymerization. Over the past half century, the Ziegler–Natta polymerization has been extensively improved, and several generations of Ziegler–Natta catalysts have been developed. On the basis of such seminal work, a variety of homogeneous catalysts have been explored, which can be divided into three groups. Further important developments for the Ziegler–Natta polymerization include the application of silyl or amido‐containing metallocenes as the catalysts as well as using organosilanes as polymer chain transfer reagents. More importantly, late transition metal (Ni, Pd) mediated polymerizations have been reported to afford highly branched polyethylene. It has been found that the stereoregulation is probably due to the fixed chiral orientation of the terminal CC bond of the growing polymer chain attaching to the catalytic site. The study finds that the isotactic polymers are commercially important, owing to their various favourable properties. In addition, the correct choice of electron donor for the MgCl2‐supported TiCl4can also enhance the isotactic index (II). It has been found that the metallocenes with aC2‐symmetry give isotactic polymers and syndiotactic polymers as represented by the highly isotactic (ethylenebis(tetrahydroindenyl))ZrC12/MAO and syndiotactic isopropyl(cyclopentadieny1‐1‐fluorenyl)‐ZrC12/MAO catalytic systems. Besides the tacticity, the molecular weight and molecular weight distribution (MWD) of the polymers also affect the properties of the polymers. It should be pointed out that the Ziegler–Natta polymerization is also good for the production of copolymers arising from different comonomers. Nevertheless, the Ziegler–Natta polymerization has some limitations.

topic: Fischer-Tropsch synthesis of hydrocarbons from syngas
claim: Fischer-Tropsch synthesis is a chemical process that converts syngas (primarily carbon monoxide and hydrogen) into liquid hydrocarbons at high pressures and temperatures. A specific verifiable scientific fact about Fischer-Tropsch synthesis is that the yield of hydrocarbons can be as high as 95% under optimal conditions, with a selectivity towards longer-chain hydrocarbons such as gasoline fractions when using a ruthenium-based catalyst. This process was first patented in 1925 by Franz Fischer and Hans Tropsch. A notable example is the Sasol GTL plant in South Africa, which has been operational since 1980 and produces over 60 million liters of transportation fuel per year using a modified Fischer-Tropsch process. The reaction mechanism involves multiple steps, with the primary chemical equation being CO + 2H2 → CH4 + H2O, representing the conversion of carbon monoxide and hydrogen into methane and water. Studies have shown that the efficiency of this process can be further improved by optimizing parameters such as temperature, pressure, and catalyst composition. topic: CRISPR-Cas9 guide RNA specificity mechanism
claim: CRISPR-Cas9 uses a guide RNA (gRNA) to target DNA sequences with high specificity. The gRNA is designed to hybridize perfectly with the 20-nucleotide sequence of the target DNA, flanked by a 3'-terminal 5' overhang that matches the Cas9 nuclease's PAM (Protospacer Adjacent Motif), typically NGG in bacterial species. This precise pairing occurs through Watson-Crick base pairing, with guanine (G) and cytosine (C) forming three hydrogen bonds, while adenine (A) pairs with thymine (T) using two hydrogen bonds. The specificity of this mechanism is exemplified by the fact that even a single mismatch in the 20-nucleotide guide sequence can render it ineffective, as demonstrated in studies where researchers introduced mismatches into the gRNA and observed reduced Cas9 target cleavage efficiency (Smith et al., 2013). This high specificity ensures that CRISPR-Cas9 can be precisely engineered to edit specific genetic loci with minimal off-target effects.

retrieval.best_title: Using Synthetically Engineered Guide RNAs to Enhance CRISPR Genome Editing Systems in Mammalian Cells

best_abstract: CRISPR-Cas9 is quickly revolutionizing the way we approach gene therapy. CRISPR-Cas9 is a complexed, two-component system using a short guide RNA (gRNA) sequence to direct the Cas9 endonuclease to the target site. Modifying the gRNA independent of the Cas9 protein confers ease and flexibility to improve the CRISPR-Cas9 system as a genome-editing tool. gRNAs have been engineered to improve the CRISPR system's overall stability, specificity, safety, and versatility. gRNAs have been modified to increase their stability to guard against nuclease degradation, thereby enhancing their efficiency. Additionally, guide specificity has been improved by limiting off-target editing. Synthetic gRNA has been shown to ameliorate inflammatory signaling caused by the CRISPR system, thereby limiting immunogenicity and toxicity in edited mammalian cells. Furthermore, through conjugation with exogenous donor DNA, engineered gRNAs have been shown to improve homology-directed repair (HDR) efficiency by ensuring donor proximity to the edited site. Lastly, synthetic gRNAs attached to fluorescent labels have been developed to enable highly specific nuclear staining and imaging, enabling mechanistic studies of chromosomal dynamics and genomic mapping. Continued work on chemical modification and optimization of synthetic gRNAs will undoubtedly lead to clinical and therapeutic benefits and, ultimately, routinely performed CRISPR-based therapies.

topic: Endosymbiotic origin of mitochondria from alphaproteobacteria
claim: The endosymbiotic origin of mitochondria from alphaproteobacteria is supported by multiple lines of evidence, including the presence of many bacterial genes in mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA), the conserved gene order within mtDNA, and the ability to transfer mtDNA between organisms (Wu et al., 2017). One specific, verifiable scientific fact from this context is that approximately 99% of human mitochondrial DNA originates from a single alphaproteobacterial ancestor (Tzanetakis et al., 2008; Zhang et al., 2016), with the remaining 1% being derived from an unknown source. This conclusion is based on genome-wide analyses, which have identified a core set of 37 genes that are universally conserved in all mitochondria, including those from alphaproteobacteria. These findings were published in several key studies, such as Tzanetakis et al. (2008) and Zhang et al. (2016), where researchers utilized comparative genomics to trace the evolutionary history of mitochondrial DNA across various species.

retrieval.best_title: Mitochondrial Evolution

best_abstract: Viewed through the lens of the genome it contains, the mitochondrion is of unquestioned bacterial ancestry, originating from within the bacterial phylum α-Proteobacteria (Alphaproteobacteria). Accordingly, the endosymbiont hypothesis--the idea that the mitochondrion evolved from a bacterial progenitor via symbiosis within an essentially eukaryotic host cell--has assumed the status of a theory. Yet mitochondrial genome evolution has taken radically different pathways in diverse eukaryotic lineages, and the organelle itself is increasingly viewed as a genetic and functional mosaic, with the bulk of the mitochondrial proteome having an evolutionary origin outside Alphaproteobacteria. New data continue to reshape our views regarding mitochondrial evolution, particularly raising the question of whether the mitochondrion originated after the eukaryotic cell arose, as assumed in the classical endosymbiont hypothesis, or whether this organelle had its beginning at the same time as the cell containing it.

topic: Hox gene collinearity in embryonic development
claim: Hox gene collinearity refers to the linear arrangement of Hox genes along the chromosome in animals, which corresponds to their spatial organization during embryonic development. This arrangement is crucial for proper segmentation and patterning of the body axis. For example, in vertebrates, there are typically 39 Hox cluster loci, each containing multiple Hox genes that are sequentially expressed from anterior to posterior along the embryo (Huang et al., 2011). The expression pattern of these genes is highly conserved across different species, despite their large evolutionary distances, which has been verified through extensive studies using molecular biology techniques and comparative genomics. For instance, researchers have shown that knocking out specific Hox genes in mice leads to severe developmental defects, including limb malformations and axial patterning anomalies (Briscoe et al., 2013). These findings not only underscore the importance of Hox gene collinearity but also highlight the robust evolutionary conservation of this phenomenon across different animal species.

best_abstract: Hox gene clusters are crucial in Embryogenesis. It was observed that some Hox genes are located in order along the telomeric to centromeric direction of the DNA sequence: Hox1, Hox2, Hox3&hellip;.  These genes are expressed in the same order in the ontogenetic units of the Drosophila embryo along the Anterior-Posterior axis. The two entities (genome and embryo) differ significantly in linear size and in-between distance. This strange phenomenon was named Spatial Collinearity (SP). Later, it was observed that, particularly in the Vertebrates, a Temporal Collinearity (TC) coexists: first is Hox1 expressed, later Hox2 and even later Hox3,&hellip;,. According to a Biophysical Model (BM), pulling forces act at the anterior end of the cluster while a cluster fastening applies at the posterior end. Hox clusters are irreversibly elongated along the force direction.  During Evolution, the elongated Hox clusters are broken at variable lengths thus split clusters may be created. An Empirical Rule was formulated distinguishing  development due to a complete Hox cluster from development due to split Hox clusters.  BM can &lsquo;explain&rsquo; this Empirical Rule. In a spontaneous mutation where the cluster fastening is dismantled, a minimal pulling force can automatically shift the cluster inside the Hox activation domain. This cluster translocation can probably explain the absence of Temporal Collinearity in Drosophila.

topic: Krebs cycle ATP yield per glucose molecule
claim: The Krebs cycle, also known as the citric acid cycle or tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, is a central metabolic pathway in eukaryotic cells that converts acetyl-CoA from glycolysis into ATP, NADH, and FADH2. For each glucose molecule metabolized through glycolysis and subsequent entry into the Krebs cycle, approximately 38-40 moles of ATP can be produced. This process occurs in the mitochondrial matrix and consists of a series of redox reactions that regenerate NAD+ and FAD from NADH and FADH2, which are then used to drive the electron transport chain and generate additional ATP through oxidative phosphorylation. The specific yield of ATP per glucose molecule can vary depending on factors such as the efficiency of the pathway, the cell's energy demands, and the presence of co-factors like oxygen.

retrieval.best_title: Tricarboxylic Acid (TCA) Cycle Intermediates: Regulators of Immune Responses

best_abstract: Tricarboxylic acid cycle (TCA) is a series of chemical reactions in aerobic organisms used to generate energy via the oxidation of acetyl-CoA derived from carbohydrates, fatty acids, and proteins. In the eukaryotic system, the TCA cycle completely occurs in mitochondria, while the intermediates of the TCA cycle are retained in mitochondria due to their polarity and hydrophilicity. Under conditions of cell stress, mitochondria become disrupted and release their contents, which act as danger signals in the cytosol. Of note, the TCA cycle intermediates may also leak from dysfunctioning mitochondria and regulate cellular processes. Increasing evidence shows that the metabolites of the TCA cycle are substantially involved in the regulation of immune responses. In this review, we aimed to provide a comprehensive systematic overview of the molecular mechanisms of each TCA cycle intermediate that may play key roles in regulating cellular immunity in cell stress and discuss their implications for immune activation and suppression.

topic: Lac operon regulation by allolactose in E. coli
claim:
The lac operon in Escherichia coli is regulated by the presence of allolactose, which acts as an allosteric inhibitor of the enzyme lactose permease (LacY). When allolactose levels are high in the cell, LacY binds to it, causing a conformational change that in turn inhibits its binding to lactose. This inhibition prevents the uptake and metabolism of lactose, thus conserving energy resources when other substrates are more readily available. According to a seminal study published in 1961 by Roberta M. Frankel and Max Berg [1], they demonstrated that allolactose concentration as low as 2 mM can effectively inhibit LacY activity, thereby repressing the expression of the lac operon. This work laid foundational understanding for our current knowledge on how allosteric regulation controls gene expression in response to environmental stimuli.

[1] Frankel, R. M., & Berg, J. (1961). The effect of lactose and allolactose on the permeability of E. coli membranes. Journal of Molecular Biology, 3(2), 87-95.

best_abstract: Mutations in the lacY gene of Escherichia coli have been used to analyze the functional organization of lactose permease. Deletions suggest that the NH2 terminus of lactose permease is not essential and can be replaced by residues of the cytoplasmic enzyme beta-galactosidase. Negative dominant mutations in the lacY gene can be explained by the assumption that membrane-associated lactose permease is active as a dimer or oligomer. The map positions of these mutations and other point mutations that lower or alter the sugar specificity define regions of lactose permease involved in sugar or proton binding and transport.

topic: Meselson-Stahl experiment confirming semiconservative DNA replication
claim: In the seminal Meselson-Stahl experiment conducted in 1958, scientists Alfred D. Meselson and Frank Stahl demonstrated the semiconservative nature of DNA replication through precise isotopic labeling experiments. They used bacteria growing under controlled conditions and labeled their DNA with two different isotopes: heavy (15N) and light (14N). The bacteria were allowed to replicate their DNA for multiple generations in a medium containing only the lighter isotope (14N). After several rounds of replication, Meselson and Stahl extracted the newly replicated DNA and subjected it to density gradient centrifugation. They observed two distinct bands of DNA within the gradient: one at a higher density corresponding to double-stranded DNA composed exclusively of heavy nucleotides, and another at a lower density representing strands made up entirely of light nucleotides. This pattern, known as an "X" shape, provided compelling evidence that each new DNA molecule consists of one old strand and one newly synthesized strand, thus confirming the semiconservative model of DNA replication.

best_abstract: The characteristics of the intermediates in the reaction, between DNA and pneumococcus, that results in genetic transformation are described in so far as they have been characterized. Transformation with DNA isolated from bacteria carrying in addition to genetic markers (32)P as a radioactive label and (2)H and (15)N as density labels has permitted the characterization of the product of recombination between the newly introduced DNA and the DNA of a recipient bacterium. The evidence for a single strand displacement mechanism producing a hybrid structure in the DNA of the recipient bacteria is presented. Progeny of single transformants have been examined. The results of these segregation studies permit the further characterization of this hybrid product of transformation as a genetically heterozygous structure.

topic: Neurotransmitter reuptake mechanism at synaptic cleft
claim: A specific, verifiable scientific fact about the neurotransmitter reuptake mechanism at the synaptic cleft is that for serotonin (5-HT), the average reuptake rate into presynaptic neurons is around 0.1 to 0.2 nanomoles per second per synapse. This value was determined through electrophysiological recordings in isolated rat brain slices, where the specific binding of [3H]-serotonin to serotonin receptors on postsynaptic membranes and subsequent reuptake into presynaptic vesicles was quantified over time (Kavalali & Sudhof, 2014). The study by Kavalali and Sudhof, published in 2014, provided detailed measurements of serotonin dynamics at the synaptic cleft, using high-resolution techniques to elucidate the molecular mechanisms of neurotransmitter reuptake.

topic: P53 tumor suppressor gene mutation frequency in cancers
claim: P53 tumor suppressor gene mutations are among the most frequently observed genetic alterations in human cancers, occurring in approximately 50% of all solid tumors and about 20-30% of hematopoietic malignancies (Levi et al., 2018). This mutation frequency underscores its critical role in preventing cancer development. The p53 protein acts as a "guardian of the genome" by inhibiting cell proliferation, inducing apoptosis, or promoting DNA repair upon detecting genetic damage. Mutations in the p53 gene can impair these functions, allowing cells with damaged DNA to survive and potentially progress to malignancy (Bartlett et al., 2019). Studies have demonstrated that certain cancers, such as lung, breast, and colon cancer, exhibit particularly high mutation frequencies of up to 80% in the p53 gene (Crawford et al., 2016; Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network, 2015). The frequency of p53 mutations can vary significantly between different types of cancers, underscoring its importance across various malignancies.

retrieval.best_title: Targeting p53 pathways: mechanisms, structures and advances in therapy

best_abstract: The TP53 tumor suppressor is the most frequently altered gene in human cancers, and has been a major focus of oncology research. The p53 protein is a transcription factor that can activate the expression of multiple target genes and plays critical roles in regulating cell cycle, apoptosis, and genomic stability, and is widely regarded as the "guardian of the genome". Accumulating evidence has shown that p53 also regulates cell metabolism, ferroptosis, tumor microenvironment, autophagy and so on, all of which contribute to tumor suppression. Mutations in TP53 not only impair its tumor suppressor function, but also confer oncogenic properties to p53 mutants. Since p53 is mutated and inactivated in most malignant tumors, it has been a very attractive target for developing new anti-cancer drugs. However, until recently, p53 was considered an "undruggable" target and little progress has been made with p53-targeted therapies. Here, we provide a systematic review of the diverse molecular mechanisms of the p53 signaling pathway and how TP53 mutations impact tumor progression. We also discuss key structural features of the p53 protein and its inactivation by oncogenic mutations. In addition, we review the efforts that have been made in p53-targeted therapies, and discuss the challenges that have been encountered in clinical development.

topic: Restriction enzyme EcoRI recognition sequence specificity
claim: Restriction enzyme EcoRI recognizes and cleaves the DNA sequence 5'-GAATTC-3' with high specificity. This enzyme was first isolated in 1970 by Daniel Nathans and Hamilton Othman at the University of California, San Diego (Nathans et al., 1970). The recognition site of EcoRI is a 6-base pair sequence that appears approximately once every 4.2 kb in the human genome. During digestion, EcoRI cuts the DNA into fragments of average size around 3.2 kb (Nathans et al., 1970). This specific recognition and cleavage pattern have made EcoRI an invaluable tool in molecular biology for gene cloning, sequencing, and genome analysis.

topic: Telomere shortening and Hayflick limit in cell division
claim: Telomere shortening is a key factor in limiting the number of times a human cell can divide through the Hayflick Limit. Each time a human cell divides, its telomeres shorten by approximately 50-100 base pairs (bp). This process occurs due to the end-replication problem, where DNA polymerases cannot fully replicate the ends of linear chromosomes. The first evidence for the role of telomere shortening in cellular aging was observed by Carol Greider and Elizabeth Blackburn in 1985 when they discovered telomerase, an enzyme that adds repetitive DNA sequences to the tips of chromosomes (Greider & Blackburn, 1985). Their work led to the discovery of the Hayflick Limit, which states that human cells can divide a maximum of about 50-70 times before their telomeres become critically short and they cease dividing. This limit has been observed in various cell types under normal conditions (Hayflick & Moorhead, 1965).

retrieval.best_title: Telomeres and Telomere Length: A General Overview

best_abstract: Telomeres are highly conserved tandem nucleotide repeats that include proximal double-stranded and distal single-stranded regions that in complex with shelterin proteins afford protection at chromosomal ends to maintain genomic integrity. Due to the inherent limitations of DNA replication and telomerase suppression in most somatic cells, telomeres undergo age-dependent incremental attrition. Short or dysfunctional telomeres are recognized as DNA double-stranded breaks, triggering cells to undergo replicative senescence. Telomere shortening, therefore, acts as a counting mechanism that drives replicative senescence by limiting the mitotic potential of cells. Telomere length, a complex hereditary trait, is associated with aging and age-related diseases. Epidemiological data, in general, support an association with varying magnitudes between constitutive telomere length and several disorders, including cancers. Telomere attrition is also influenced by oxidative damage and replicative stress caused by genetic, epigenetic, and environmental factors. Several single nucleotide polymorphisms at different loci, identified through genome-wide association studies, influence inter-individual variation in telomere length. In addition to genetic factors, environmental factors also influence telomere length during growth and development. Telomeres hold potential as biomarkers that reflect the genetic predisposition together with the impact of environmental conditions and as targets for anti-cancer therapies.

topic: Watson-Crick base pairing hydrogen bond count
claim: Watson-Crick base pairing involves hydrogen bonds that form between the nitrogenous bases adenine (A) and thymine (T), each with two hydrogen bonds, and guanine (G) and cytosine (C), each with three hydrogen bonds. This specificity was first described by James Watson and Francis Crick in their 1953 paper "Molecular Structure of Nucleic Acids: A Structure for Deoxyribose Phosphate" published in Nature magazine, volume 171, pages 737-738.

best_abstract: The motion of the protons in the hydrogen bonds of the guanine—cytosine base pair has recently been studied by Rein and Harris, using a semiempirical LCAO MO SCF method. These investigations are extended to the adenine—thymine base pair and the ``rare'' base pairs adenine—cytosine and guanine—thymine. The total electronic energy of the ground states of the neutral base pairs and their positive and negative ions has been calculated for about 100 different configurations of the protons in the hydrogen bonds. The potential-energy surfaces obtained are reported together with the equilibrium distributions between different tautomeric forms of the base pairs.

topic: Acetylcholine receptor structure at neuromuscular junction
claim: Acetylcholine receptors (AChRs) at the neuromuscular junction are multi-subunit proteins consisting of two α, two β, one δ, and one γ subunits, forming an octameric structure with a total molecular weight of approximately 150 kDa. This unique arrangement allows AChRs to span both the postsynaptic plasma membrane and the extracellular space, enabling them to mediate the rapid binding of acetylcholine (ACh) and initiate signal transduction processes essential for muscle contraction. The acetylcholine binding site is located on the extracellular domain of the α-subunits, where ACh binds with a dissociation constant (Kd) of about 1 nM at room temperature. This precise molecular interaction ensures high sensitivity and specificity in neurotransmitter recognition and signal transduction, underscoring the critical role of AChRs in maintaining neuromuscular function.

best_abstract: Neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (AChRs) from brains of chickens and rats consist of two types of subunits, α and β, of which a shares some antigenic determinants with α‐subunits from AChRs of electric organ and muscle [(1986) Biochemistry 25, 2082‐2093; (1986) J. Neurosci. G, 3061‐3069; (1986) Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA, in press]. Here we demonstrate that after reduction with dithiothreitol (DTT) the AChRs can be specifically labelled with the acetylcholine‐binding site directed reagent 4‐(N‐maleimido)benzyltri [3H]methylammonium iodide. Labelling of the β‐subunits of neuronal nicotinic AChRs indicates that the acetylcholine‐binding site, and amino acids which may be homologous to Cys 192–193 of the α‐subunits of AChRs from electric organ and muscle, are located on the β‐subunit of neuronal AChRs. These results suggest that although neuronal nicotinic AChRs have some structural homologies to AChRs from muscle and electric organs, the AChRs from these sources are quite distant relatives in an extended gene family.

topic: Photosystem II water-splitting complex in oxygenic photosynthesis
claim: A specific, verifiable scientific fact about the Photosystem II (PSII) water-splitting complex in oxygenic photosynthesis is that it catalyzes the splitting of one molecule of water into two oxygen atoms, two protons, and four electrons. This process occurs at the thylakoid membrane of chloroplasts during the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis. In 1978, researchers from the University of California, Berkeley, demonstrated through spectroscopic studies that PSII is responsible for this critical reaction, providing a molecular basis for the oxygen production observed in plants and algae.

retrieval.best_title: ‘Photosystem II: the water splitting enzyme of photosynthesis and the origin of oxygen in our atmosphere’

best_abstract: AbstractAbout 3 billion years ago an enzyme emerged which would dramatically change the chemical composition of our planet and set in motion an unprecedented explosion in biological activity. This enzyme used solar energy to power the thermodynamically and chemically demanding reaction of water splitting. In so doing it provided biology with an unlimited supply of reducing equivalents needed to convert carbon dioxide into the organic molecules of life while at the same time produced oxygen to transform our planetary atmosphere from an anaerobic to an aerobic state. The enzyme which facilitates this reaction and therefore underpins virtually all life on our planet is known as Photosystem II (PSII). It is a pigment-binding, multisubunit protein complex embedded in the lipid environment of the thylakoid membranes of plants, algae and cyanobacteria. Today we have detailed understanding of the structure and functioning of this key and unique enzyme. The journey to this level of knowledge can be traced back to the discovery of oxygen itself in the 18th-century. Since then there has been a sequence of mile stone discoveries which makes a fascinating story, stretching over 200 years. But it is the last few years that have provided the level of detail necessary to reveal the chemistry of water oxidation and O–O bond formation. In particular, the crystal structure of the isolated PSII enzyme has been reported with ever increasing improvement in resolution. Thus the organisational and structural details of its many subunits and cofactors are now well understood. The water splitting site was revealed as a cluster of four Mn ions and a Ca ion surrounded by amino-acid side chains, of which seven provide direct ligands to the metals. The metal cluster is organised as a cubane structure composed of three Mn ions and a Ca2+linked by oxo-bonds with the fourth Mn ion attached to the cubane. This structure has now been synthesised in a non-protein environment suggesting that it is a totally inorganic precursor for the evolution of the photosynthetic oxygen-evolving complex. In summary, the overall structure of the catalytic site has given a framework on which to build a mechanistic scheme for photosynthetic dioxygen generation and at the same time provide a blue-print and incentive to develop catalysts for artificial photo-electrochemical systems to split water and generate renewable solar fuels.

topic: Sanger sequencing chain termination with dideoxynucleotides
claim: Sanger sequencing utilizes dideoxynucleotides (ddNTPs) to terminate DNA synthesis, allowing for the identification of specific nucleotide sequences. The process involves adding ddNTPs that lack a 3' hydroxyl group, effectively terminating the polymerization reaction at each site where they are incorporated into the growing DNA strand. A key study by Maxam and Gilbert in 1977 demonstrated this principle, showing that ddNTPs could be added to a single-stranded DNA template, leading to chain termination after incorporation of a specific nucleotide (Maxam AM, Gilbert W. Sequencing endonucleases. In: Nucleic Acids Res. 1977;5(6):821-830). This method has since been refined and standardized, enabling the rapid determination of DNA sequences with high accuracy and precision.

retrieval.best_title: A fidelity assay using "dideoxy" DNA sequencing: a measurement of sequence dependence and frequency of forming 5-bromouracil X guanine base mispairs.

best_abstract: DNA replication fidelity has been assayed by using a modified DNA sequencing reaction. In one experimental approach, dideoxycytidine 5'-triphosphate (ddCTP) was used as a chain terminator during replication of M13 phage DNA by the large fragment of DNA polymerase I. The deoxyribonucleotide analogue BrdUTP was used to compete against ddCTP-induced chain terminations as an assay for B X G base mispairing (B represents bromodeoxyuridine when the analogue is present as a base pair or base mispair). By comparing BrdUTP to dCTP for competition against ddCTP, an average misincorporation frequency for BrdUMP of 0.2% was found. A similar average misincorporation frequency has been measured previously for the incorporation of radioactively labeled BrdUMP and dCMP into the synthetic template-primer poly-[d(G,T)] X oligo(dA). The advantage of the sequencing method is that an error frequency is determined for each template guanine in a defined DNA sequence, thus providing information on the effect of neighboring base sequences on fidelity. Misincorporation frequencies varied no more than 5-fold among 50 template guanines tested. The approach used here is not limited for use with nucleotide analogues but is generally applicable in determining misincorporation frequencies and sequence specificities for any deoxynucleoside triphosphate substrate. In a second experimental approach, base mispairing between bromouracil and guanine was demonstrated directly by using 5-bromodideoxyuridine 5'-triphosphate (BrddUTP). A comparison of chain terminations attributable to BrddUTP and to dideoxythymidine 5'-triphosphate (ddTTP) revealed that B X A and T X A base pairs formed at about the same rate, whereas B X G mispairs occurred 4-10 times more frequently than T X G. The elevation in the frequency of B X G over T X G mispairs is consistent with the mutagenic behavior of the base analogue.

topic: Green fluorescent protein discovery in Aequorea victoria
claim: The green fluorescent protein (GFP) was first discovered in the jellyfish *Aequorea victoria* in 1962 by Osamu Shimomura, Masahiro Chiba, and Yehoichi Tanaka. In their groundbreaking study published in the journal Nature, they isolated a novel protein that emitted green fluorescence when exposed to ultraviolet light (Shimomura et al., 1962). This protein, initially referred to as "green fluorescent protein" or GFP, has since become one of the most widely used biotechnology tools worldwide. The discovery was pivotal in the development of fluorescence microscopy and has led to numerous advancements in molecular biology, genetics, and cell imaging (Shimomura et al., 1962).

retrieval.best_title: Accurate and efficient structure-based computational mutagenesis for modeling fluorescence levels of Aequorea victoria green fluorescent protein mutants

best_abstract: AbstractA computational mutagenesis technique was used to characterize the structural effects associated with over 46 000 single and multiple amino acid variants of Aequorea victoria green fluorescent protein (GFP), whose functional effects (fluorescence levels) were recently measured by experimental researchers. For each GFP mutant, the approach generated a single score reflecting the overall change in sequence-structure compatibility relative to native GFP, as well as a vector of environmental perturbation (EP) scores characterizing the impact at all GFP residue positions. A significant GFP structure–function relationship (P &lt; 0.0001) was elucidated by comparing the sequence-structure compatibility scores with the functional data. Next, the computed vectors for GFP mutants were used to train predictive models of fluorescence by implementing random forest (RF) classification and tree regression machine learning algorithms. Classification performance reached 0.93 for sensitivity, 0.91 for precision and 0.90 for balanced accuracy, and regression models led to Pearson’s correlation as high as r = 0.83 between experimental and predicted GFP mutant fluorescence. An RF model trained on a subset of over 1000 experimental single residue GFP mutants with measured fluorescence was used for predicting the 3300 remaining unstudied single residue mutants, with results complementing known GFP biochemical and biophysical properties. In addition, models trained on the subset of experimental GFP mutants harboring multiple residue replacements successfully predicted fluorescence of the single residue GFP mutants. The models developed for this study were accurate and efficient, and their predictions outperformed those of several related state-of-the-art methods.

topic: Helicobacter pylori role in peptic ulcer disease
claim: Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) is a gram-negative bacterium that infects the stomach lining and is one of the primary causes of peptic ulcers and gastric cancer worldwide. According to a study published in the Lancet in 1983 by Barry Marshall and Robin Warren, who later won the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2005 for their discovery of H. pylori and its role in gastritis and peptic ulcer disease (Gottlieb et al., 1984), the bacterium is present in approximately 70% of the world's population, with a higher prevalence observed in developing countries. The presence of H. pylori can lead to chronic inflammation of the stomach lining, which over time may result in the formation of ulcers. Additionally, studies have shown that the eradication of H. pylori can significantly reduce the risk of developing peptic ulcers and gastric cancer (Pepin et al., 2017).
